paragraphs: I sit at the junction of business strategy and engineering execution. My work is translating ambiguous business problems into precise technical directions — and making sure the direction survives contact with implementation.

I have worked with founders building their first platform and with enterprises rearchitecting systems that serve millions. The skill is the same in both cases: figuring out what actually matters, then building only that.
about.callout: Strategy without execution is fiction. I do both.

metrics.label: Average reduction in planned scope after discovery
Industries advised: retail, fintech, health, SaaS

expertise: Build vs. buy vs. integrate decisions

engagements.context: Series B SaaS company with a legacy monolith blocking product velocity
engagements.challenge: Engineering team spending 60% of sprint capacity on maintenance and firefighting
engagements.outcome: Delivered a migration roadmap and team restructure; velocity improved 2× within two quarters

engagements.context: Retail group evaluating a full commerce platform replacement
engagements.challenge: Vendor selection across six platforms with conflicting stakeholder priorities
engagements.outcome: Ran structured RFP and proof-of-concept process; saved £2M vs. initial shortlist

engagements.context: HealthTech startup building a patient data platform from scratch
engagements.challenge: Founder with a strong product vision but no engineering team or architecture
engagements.outcome: Defined MVP scope, selected tech stack, hired founding engineers — live in 9 months

engagements.context: PE-backed retail business post-acquisition requiring technical due diligence
engagements.challenge: Assess risk in an inherited codebase with no documentation and a departing team
engagements.outcome: Delivered risk report and remediation roadmap; informed final acquisition terms

perspective: Most technology problems are prioritisation problems. The system is usually fixable — the real question is whether you are fixing the right thing.
